message:
fix(net): eliminate waker-while-locked hazards in TCP and WebSocket split halves

TCP split (split.rs): When set_interest returns NotConnected, the old
code woke only the current task via cx.waker(). Both the read and write
halves share a single registration, so when it is dropped both waiters
must be notified — otherwise the other half silently stalls. The fix
wakes both stored wakers individually before returning. Adds a
not_connected_modify_wakes_both_split_waiters test with CountingWaker
to verify both halves are woken.

Virtual TCP (virtual_tcp.rs): poll_write, poll_shutdown, and Drop were
calling waker.wake() while still holding the Mutex guard. If the woken
task immediately tries to re-acquire the same lock on the same thread
(or if the waker callback contends the lock), this is a potential
deadlock. The fix extracts the waker into a local before dropping the
guard, then wakes outside the critical section. Applied consistently
across write, shutdown, drop, and TcpStreamApi::shutdown paths.

WebSocket split (split.rs): Concurrent send_frame calls from the read
half (pong replies) and write half could byte-interleave on the
underlying IO because both sides independently loop over poll_write.
Adds a SplitWritePermit RAII guard acquired via acquire_write_permit()
that serializes frame-level writes through a writer_active flag +
writer_waiter waker in the shared state. Includes InterleavingIo test
mock that yields after every byte and a
split_writes_do_not_interleave_frame_bytes test proving full-frame
atomicity under concurrent sends.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>
